---
title: "![](images/icons/icon_11.png){.title-icon} Long ↔ wide format"
subtitle: "Wide vs. long, and pivots with pivot_wider() / pivot_longer()"
date: last-modified
---

The registers you extract from are almost always in *long format* with many rows per person, while your analysis dataset needs to be in *wide format* with one row per person. This page explains the difference and shows how to switch between them with `pivot_wider()` and `pivot_longer()`.

::: {.callout-note}
In practice you usually reduce long register data to one row per person with `group_by() + slice()` (see [12a — Link your extracts](12a_joins.qmd)). You use pivots when you explicitly want **several time points side by side** as separate columns.
:::

---

## Wide vs. long format

Imagine three persons with weight measurements at two time points.

**Wide format** — one row per person, one column per time point:

| pnr | weight_baseline | weight_6mo |
|---|---|---|
| 001 | 120 | 95 |
| 002 | 105 | 88 |
| 003 | 98 | 80 |

**Long format** — one row per observation, one column for time point and one for value:

| pnr | timepoint | weight |
|---|---|---|
| 001 | baseline | 120 |
| 001 | 6mo | 95 |
| 002 | baseline | 105 |
| 002 | 6mo | 88 |
| 003 | baseline | 98 |
| 003 | 6mo | 80 |

Both formats contain **the same information** — they are just arranged differently.

---

## Which formats are where?

| Register | Format | Why |
|---|---|---|
| BEF | Long | One row per person **per snapshot** — quarterly since 2008, otherwise annual |
| LPR (lpr_adm + lpr_diag) | Long | One row per **diagnosis per contact** — one admission can have 10 diagnoses |
| LMDB | Long | One row per **dispensed prescription** — the same person can have hundreds |
| DODSAARS | Wide | One row per **deceased person** — already one-per-person |
| Your analysis dataset | Wide | One row per person — one column per covariate |

**Registers are usually in long format. Your analysis dataset should typically be in wide format.**

---

## Pivots — switch between long and wide

### Long → wide with `pivot_wider()`

```r
library(tidyr)   # pivot_wider, pivot_longer

df_wide <- df_long %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from  = "timepoint",  # which column should become column names?
    values_from = "weight"      # which column contains the values?
  )
```

::: {.callout-warning}
**You CANNOT `pivot_wider()` an entire LPR table.**
LPR potentially has hundreds of unique ICD codes per person — `pivot_wider()` would give you one column per code, i.e. thousands of columns and a dataset that is impossible to work with.

Instead use the pattern from [Phase 9](09b_udtraek_fra_lpr.qmd): extract the first date for one specific diagnosis group, save as `extract_dementia.rds`, and join it as one column with `left_join()`. One outcome variable = one column = one RDS file.
:::

<details>
<summary>Practical example: employment status from AKM — long to wide</summary>

AKM (the Labour Classification Module) stores one row per person **per year** — long format. If you want to compare employment status at baseline and follow-up side by side, you can pivot:

```r
library(dplyr)   # filter, mutate
library(tidyr)   # pivot_wider

# AKM in long format — two years per person:
akm_long <- data.frame(
  pnr   = c("001", "001", "002", "002", "003", "003"),
  aar   = c(2015, 2020, 2015, 2020, 2015, 2020),
  socio = c(110, 110, 210, 310, 150, 150)   # employment categories from AKM (socio13)
)

# Convert to wide — one column per year:
akm_wide <- akm_long %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from   = aar,
    values_from  = socio,
    names_prefix = "socio_"   # gives columns: socio_2015, socio_2020
  )

# Result:
#   pnr   socio_2015  socio_2020
#   001          110         110
#   002          210         310
#   003          150         150
```

In practice you would typically not pivot AKM — you filter to one specific year with `filter(aar == index_year)` and use `group_by() + slice()` ([12a](12a_joins.qmd)). Pivot is relevant when you explicitly want two time points side by side in the analysis.

</details>

### Wide → long with `pivot_longer()`

```r
df_long <- df_wide %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = c(weight_baseline, weight_6mo),  # columns to "fold in"
    names_to  = "timepoint",                     # name for the new timepoint column
    values_to = "weight"                         # name for the new value column
  )
```

---

## Next steps

Once your extracts are in the right format, you link them into one analysis-ready dataset:

→ [12a — Link your extracts](12a_joins.qmd)

## See also

- [12a — Link your extracts](12a_joins.qmd) — `group_by() + slice()`, joins and `bind_rows()`
- [Functions: overview](15a_guide_til_funktioner.qmd) — all functions for transforming data
